29033327.c0.paper_title: Natural Language Inference from Multiple Premises

contribution_obj.name: Formulation of the Multiple Premise Entailment (MPE) task
contribution_obj.description: The paper defines a new textual entailment task, Multiple Premise Entailment (MPE), in which each example consists of an unordered set of four independently written image captions (premises) and a single hypothesis sentence. The hypothesis must be classified as entailment, neutral, or contradiction with respect to the combined information from all premises. This formulation extends standard single‑premise natural language inference by requiring aggregation across multiple sentences, handling cross‑sentence coreference and event resolution, and by deliberately limiting lexical overlap between premises and hypothesis.

types.explanation: It reconceives textual entailment by defining a novel multi‑premise inference task and associated evaluation protocol.

prerequisites.name: Standard single‑premise textual entailment tasks and datasets
prerequisites.description: Foundational NLI resources such as SNLI, SICK, and the RTE challenge define the entailment/neutral/contradiction label schema, provide benchmark data for single‑premise inference, and establish crowdsourced annotation procedures.
prerequisites.explanation: Formulating a new NLI variant requires a clear understanding of the conventional task definition, label set, and evaluation methodology.

references.paper_title: A large annotated corpus for learning natural language inference
references.paper_venue: Proceedings of the 2015 Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing

The paper describes a crowdsourced data collection protocol for NLI, which is a foundational method used in standard single‑premise entailment datasets.

prerequisites.description: The ATE task introduced by Young et al. (2014) uses four image captions as a premise set and a short phrase as hypothesis, together with a denotation graph that provides hierarchical phrase reductions for hypothesis simplification.
prerequisites.explanation: MPE builds directly on the idea of a multi‑caption premise set and adopts the denotation‑graph based hypothesis generation to avoid trivial lexical entailments.

prerequisites.explanation: The four premises for each MPE item are drawn from four distinct Flickr30K captions of the same image, making the corpus a prerequisite resource for constructing the task.

prerequisites.explanation: Designing the MPE task required a labeling scheme and annotation pipeline that builds on established crowdsourcing practices used in prior NLI datasets.

14604520.c0.paper_title: A large annotated corpus for learning natural language inference

contribution_obj.name: Stanford Natural Language Inference (SNLI) Corpus
contribution_obj.description: The paper introduces SNLI, a freely available collection of 570,152 human‑written sentence pairs labeled for entailment, contradiction, and neutral (semantic independence). The pairs are generated by crowdsourcing hypotheses for image captions drawn from the Flickr30k corpus, with a balanced three‑class distribution and a validation phase involving multiple annotators to ensure high quality. SNLI includes predefined train/dev/test splits, constituency parses produced by the Stanford PCFG parser, and is released under a Creative Commons Attribution‑ShareAlike license, establishing a large‑scale benchmark for evaluating semantic representation learning and natural language inference models.

types.explanation: Provides a large, publicly released dataset of sentence pairs for training and evaluating NLI systems.
Defines standard train/dev/test splits and validation labels to serve as a benchmark for NLI performance.
Establishes a novel large‑scale crowdsourced data collection pipeline with validation.

16404002.c0.paper_title: SemEval-2014 Task 1: Evaluation of Compositional Distributional Semantic Models on Full Sentences through Semantic Relatedness and Textual Entailment

contribution_obj.name: SICK (Sentences Involving Compositional Knowledge) dataset
contribution_obj.description: The authors constructed a corpus of roughly 10,000 English sentence pairs, each annotated for (i) semantic relatedness on a 5‑point continuous scale and (ii) textual entailment (ENTAILMENT, CONTRADICTION, NEUTRAL). The pairs were generated through a controlled three‑step pipeline (normalization, expansion, pairing) to exhibit a wide range of lexical, syntactic and semantic compositional phenomena (e.g., active/passive alternations, negation, operator scope) while deliberately avoiding reliance on encyclopedic world knowledge. The resource is released both as a dataset for research and as a benchmark for evaluating compositional distributional semantic models (CDSMs) on full‑sentence semantics.

types.explanation: It provides a newly created collection of annotated sentence pairs intended for use by the research community.
The dataset is explicitly positioned as a benchmark for assessing CDSMs on full‑sentence semantic tasks.

14604520.c1.paper_title: A large annotated corpus for learning natural language inference

contribution_obj.name: Crowdsourced High-Quality NLI Data Collection Protocol
contribution_obj.description: The authors devise a reproducible crowdsourcing workflow on Amazon Mechanical Turk that presents workers with image captions from the Flickr30k corpus as premises and asks them to write three hypotheses—one entailing, one neutral, and one contradictory—thereby guaranteeing a balanced three‑class distribution. The protocol includes detailed written instructions, an evolving FAQ, qualification filters to exclude low‑quality workers, and a validation stage where each pair is re‑annotated by four additional annotators, yielding a gold label with 98 % consensus on a held‑out subset. By grounding each pair in a specific visual scenario, the design mitigates event and entity coreference ambiguities and enforces high annotation reliability.

types.explanation: Introduces a systematic, scalable crowdsourcing methodology for generating large‑scale NLI annotations.
Provides a grounding framework that reduces event and entity coreference indeterminacy in NLI data.

prerequisites.name: Existing NLI corpora and their limitations
prerequisites.description: Prior natural language inference resources such as the Recognizing Textual Entailment (RTE) challenges and the SICK dataset were small, often automatically constructed, and suffered from coreference ambiguities, motivating the need for a larger, human‑authored corpus.
prerequisites.explanation: Understanding the shortcomings of earlier NLI datasets is essential to justify and design a new, larger, high‑quality collection protocol.

prerequisites.description: A collection of roughly 160 k captions describing about 30 k images, originally gathered via crowdsourcing, providing naturalistic, literal scene descriptions that serve as premises for the NLI pairs.
prerequisites.explanation: The premises for the SNLI pairs are directly drawn from Flickr30k captions; without this resource the large‑scale, grounded data collection would not be possible.

references.paper_title: From image descriptions to visual denotations: New similarity metrics for semantic inference over event descriptions

prerequisites.description: A web‑based crowdsourcing marketplace that enables rapid recruitment of a large, diverse pool of annotators for micro‑tasks such as sentence generation and validation.
prerequisites.explanation: The entire data collection and validation pipeline relies on the ability to post HITs, enforce qualifications, and gather multiple judgments per item via MTurk.

prerequisites.name: Understanding of coreference and event/entity indeterminacy in NLI
prerequisites.description: Theoretical work showing that ambiguous coreference can lead to contradictory labeling decisions in entailment tasks, and that grounding sentences in a shared visual context can mitigate this problem.
prerequisites.explanation: The protocol’s grounding strategy and instruction design are directly motivated by prior analyses of coreference‑induced label ambiguity.

references.paper_title: Finding contradictions in text

matches.explanation: Introduces an event‑coreference filtering stage that discards sentence pairs not describing the same event, directly tackling coreference‑induced label ambiguity in inference tasks.
Proposes a topicality‑based algorithm for scoring event‑coreference alignments, providing a concrete technique to mitigate coreference ambiguity in textual inference.
Defines contradiction with explicit coreference assumptions, relating to the prerequisite but without offering a mitigation method.

references.paper_title: A SICK cure for the evaluation of compositional distributional semantic models

prerequisites.name: Annotation quality control methods (multiple annotators, consensus scoring)
prerequisites.description: Procedures for obtaining several independent judgments per item, computing majority or consensus labels, and measuring inter‑annotator agreement (e.g., Fleiss κ) to ensure reliability of the final dataset.
prerequisites.explanation: The validation phase that yields 98 % consensus depends on these established quality‑control techniques.

prerequisites.name: Instruction design and FAQ for crowdsourced linguistic tasks
prerequisites.description: Best‑practice guidelines for writing clear task instructions, providing examples, and maintaining an FAQ to address worker questions and prevent systematic errors.
prerequisites.explanation: Effective instruction and FAQ were crucial to obtaining high‑quality, diverse hypotheses from workers.

contribution_obj.name: Contradiction detection system with event‑coreference filtering
contribution_obj.description: The paper implements an end‑to‑end contradiction‑detection pipeline built on the Stanford Recognizing Textual Entailment (RTE) system. It adds an event‑coreference filtering stage that discards sentence pairs that do not describe the same event, using a root‑alignment heuristic together with an unweighted topicality‑based scoring model. The remaining pairs are processed by a rich feature extractor (polarity, numeric, antonymy, structural, factivity, modality, relational) and classified with a logistic‑regression model, producing a tool for fine‑grained contradiction detection.

types.explanation: It delivers a complete software system for automatically detecting contradictions in text.
It extends the existing RTE architecture with a novel event‑coreference filtering component.
It introduces specific algorithms for event coreference (root + topicality) and a feature‑based logistic‑regression classifier.

contribution_obj.name: Topicality‑based event‑coreference scoring method
contribution_obj.description: The paper introduces an unweighted topicality model that assigns higher weights to noun phrases appearing earlier in a sentence, using their linear position derived from typed dependency parses. A normalized topicality score is computed for each aligned noun‑phrase pair between a text and a hypothesis, and combined with a root‑alignment heuristic to decide whether the two sentences describe the same event. This filtering step improves precision of contradiction detection beyond using root‑alignment alone.

types.explanation: It proposes a novel algorithm that scores noun‑phrase alignments by positional topicality for event‑coreference estimation.
It defines a new procedural filtering stage that precedes contradiction classification.

207847663.c2-1.paper_title: ERASER : A Benchmark to Evaluate Rationalized NLP Models

contribution_obj.name: Hard-selection baseline models for rationalized predictions
contribution_obj.description: The paper implements three hard-selection architectures that produce discrete rationales and use only the extracted snippets for downstream prediction. The first adapts the Lei et al. (2016) rationalizer, training a binary mask encoder and a classifier decoder jointly with REINFORCE, using either BERT or GloVe embeddings as token encoders. The second is a pipeline that first extracts sentence-level rationales with a GRU-based encoder and then classifies using a separate model. The third is a BERT-to-BERT pipeline where a BERT encoder selects rationales and a second BERT model consumes them for prediction. All models are trained on the ERASER benchmark datasets and released as open-source code.

types.explanation: Introduces concrete model architectures that generate discrete rationales for predictions.
Provides reusable implementations of these architectures in a public code repository.

prerequisites.name: Rationalizer architecture (Lei et al., 2016)
prerequisites.description: A two-module neural model that learns a binary mask over input tokens (rationale extractor) and a classifier that consumes only the selected tokens, trained jointly via REINFORCE.
prerequisites.explanation: The hard-selection baselines directly re-implement this architecture, so understanding its design and training objective is essential.

references.paper_title: Rationalizing neural predictions
references.paper_venue: Proceedings of the 2016 Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing

matches.explanation: Defines a two‑module architecture (generator that extracts a binary mask and encoder that classifies using only the mask) and trains them jointly with a REINFORCE‑style estimator, directly mirroring the Lei et al. rationalizer.
Derives a REINFORCE‑style doubly stochastic gradient estimator for training discrete mask selections, providing the core training algorithm used in the rationalizer architecture.
Proposes concrete designs for the mask‑generator (independent and recurrent selectors), which correspond to the rationale extractor component of the rationalizer.

prerequisites.name: REINFORCE policy gradient algorithm
prerequisites.description: A Monte-Carlo gradient estimator for optimizing stochastic policies, used to train the binary mask in the rationalizer.
prerequisites.explanation: The rationalizer’s mask encoder is optimized with REINFORCE; without this algorithm the model could not be trained as described.

Provides an open‑source release of pretrained GloVe word vectors, directly supplying the static embeddings required by the source contribution.

prerequisites.name: GRU recurrent network for sentence encoding
prerequisites.description: A gated recurrent unit that processes token sequences to produce sentence-level representations.
prerequisites.explanation: The pipeline baseline that extracts sentence-level rationales uses a GRU to encode each sentence before classification.

prerequisites.explanation: All hard-selection models are trained with Adam, as described in the hyperparameter details.

contribution_obj.name: Modular encoder‑generator framework for rationale‑based prediction
contribution_obj.description: The paper proposes a high‑level conceptual framework that separates a rationale generator, which extracts a short, coherent subset of the input text, from an encoder that makes task predictions using only the selected rationale. The generator and encoder are instantiated with bidirectional RCNNs (the generator with independent or recurrent selection mechanisms, the encoder as a standard RCNN classifier). The two modules are trained jointly with a combined prediction loss and sparsity/continuity regularizer, using a REINFORCE‑style doubly stochastic gradient estimator to handle the discrete latent rationale selections. This enables accurate predictions together with interpretable justifications without any rationale annotations.

types.explanation: It defines a new modular architecture that isolates rationale generation from prediction.
It specifies concrete neural modules (generator and encoder) that implement the framework.

prerequisites.name: Latent‑variable formulation of extractive rationale generation
prerequisites.description: Formulating rationale extraction as an unsupervised latent‑variable problem where a binary mask over tokens defines the rationale, and training proceeds without any human‑provided rationales.
prerequisites.explanation: The modular framework relies on treating rationales as latent variables that must be inferred jointly with the prediction task.

references.contribution_name: Unsupervised extractive rationale generation as a latent‑variable formulation
references.explanation: Provides the problem formulation that underlies the generator‑encoder joint learning.

prerequisites.name: Generator architectures (independent and recurrent selection)
prerequisites.description: Two neural designs for the generator: (a) independent token‑wise selection using a bidirectional RCNN, and (b) recurrent selection where each decision conditions on previous selections via an additional hidden state, encouraging contiguous phrase‑level rationales.
prerequisites.explanation: These architectures constitute the concrete implementation of the generator component in the framework.

references.contribution_name: Generator variants with independent and recurrent selection mechanisms
references.explanation: Specifies the exact neural designs used for the generator.

prerequisites.name: Joint loss with sparsity and continuity regularizer
prerequisites.description: A training objective that combines (i) a prediction loss computed on the encoder output for the sampled rationale and (ii) a regularizer Ω(z)=λ₁‖z‖₁+λ₂∑ₜ|zₜ−zₜ₋₁| that penalizes the number of selected tokens and encourages contiguous selections.
prerequisites.explanation: The regularizer shapes the generator’s output to be short and coherent, which is essential for the framework’s interpretability goal.

references.contribution_name: Joint loss with sparsity and continuity regularizer for rationale selection
references.explanation: Defines the loss function that couples generator and encoder.

prerequisites.name: REINFORCE‑style doubly stochastic gradient estimator for discrete latent selections
prerequisites.description: An optimization technique that uses the REINFORCE policy‑gradient estimator to back‑propagate through the stochastic generator, sampling both minibatches and rationales, thus enabling end‑to‑end training despite the discrete nature of the rationale mask.
prerequisites.explanation: Training the modular system requires a gradient estimator for the non‑differentiable selection process.

references.contribution_name: REINFORCE‑style doubly stochastic gradient estimator for latent rationale selection
references.explanation: Provides the algorithmic foundation for learning the generator‑encoder pair.

references.paper_title: Simple statistical gradient‑following algorithms for connectionist reinforcement learning

prerequisites.name: Recurrent Convolutional Neural Network (RCNN) building block
prerequisites.description: A neural unit that combines recurrent and convolutional operations to capture non‑consecutive n‑gram features, used both in the encoder and as the base of the generator’s bidirectional network.
prerequisites.explanation: Both encoder and generator are instantiated with RCNN layers, making this architecture a prerequisite component.

references.paper_title: Molding CNNs for text: non‑linear, non‑consecutive convolutions

matches.explanation: The contribution introduces a convolutional operation that processes non‑adjacent word groups, which is tangentially related to the RCNN's goal of capturing non‑consecutive n‑gram features, but it does not combine recurrent processing.

references.paper_title: Semi‑supervised question retrieval with gated convolutions

matches.explanation: The contribution introduces a gated non‑consecutive convolutional RCNN architecture, directly providing the recurrent‑convolutional building block described in the prerequisite.

prerequisites.name: Attention mechanisms and stochastic attention (related work)
prerequisites.description: Prior models that use soft or stochastic attention to weight input tokens, providing context for why a separate rationale generator is needed.
prerequisites.explanation: The paper positions its generator as an alternative to attention‑based explanations, requiring awareness of existing attention literature.

references.paper_title: Neural machine translation by jointly learning to align and translate

matches.explanation: Introduces a soft attention mechanism that weights input tokens, directly matching the prerequisite's focus on attention mechanisms.
Describes joint end‑to‑end training of the soft attention component, providing a foundational method for using attention in models.
Analyzes learned soft alignments, which relates to attention but only tangentially to the core mechanism.

references.paper_title: Recurrent models of visual attention

matches.explanation: Introduces a stochastic location network that selects glimpse locations, i.e., a hard attention mechanism, directly matching the prerequisite's focus on stochastic attention.
Provides the REINFORCE training procedure for the stochastic location network, further supporting the prerequisite's emphasis on stochastic attention mechanisms.

prerequisites.name: Regularization techniques for sparsity (L1) and total‑variation
prerequisites.description: Standard methods that penalize the L1 norm of selection variables and the number of transitions between selected and unselected tokens to encourage short, contiguous rationales.
prerequisites.explanation: The Ω(z) regularizer builds directly on these well‑known techniques.

6468765.c0.paper_title: Semi-supervised Question Retrieval with Gated Convolutions

contribution_obj.name: Gated non-consecutive convolutional encoder (RCNN) for question representation
contribution_obj.description: The paper introduces a recurrent convolutional neural network (RCNN) that extends the non‑consecutive convolutional model of Lei et al. (2015) with an adaptive gating mechanism. At each time step a gate λ_t, computed from the current token embedding and the previous hidden state, modulates how much of the accumulated n‑gram features are retained, allowing the model to emphasize salient words and suppress noisy tokens in long question bodies. The sequence of gated hidden states is pooled (mean or last state) to produce a fixed‑dimensional question vector, and cosine similarity between vectors is used to rank candidate questions for retrieval.

types.explanation: Introduces a novel neural architecture that combines non‑consecutive convolution with learned adaptive gating.
Provides a new way to encode questions that highlights important tokens while compressing noisy text.
Adds an adaptive gating mechanism that dynamically modulates the decay factor in convolution.

11212020.c1.paper_title: Neural Machine Translation by Jointly Learning to Align and Translate

contribution_obj.name: Soft attention mechanism for differentiable alignment
contribution_obj.description: The paper introduces a soft attention mechanism that, for each target word, computes a context vector as a weighted sum of encoder annotations. The attention weights \(\alpha_{ij}\) are obtained from a feed‑forward alignment model that scores the compatibility between the decoder hidden state and each source annotation, and are normalised with a softmax. Because the whole computation is differentiable, gradients can flow through the alignment scores, allowing the attention parameters, encoder, and decoder to be trained jointly, replacing the static fixed‑length context vector of basic encoder‑decoder models.

types.explanation: It defines a new component (attention) within the encoder‑decoder architecture.
It provides an algorithmic method for computing alignment weights and context vectors.

11212020.c3.paper_title: Neural Machine Translation by Jointly Learning to Align and Translate

contribution_obj.name: Joint end‑to‑end training of attention and translation
contribution_obj.description: The paper introduces a training procedure that jointly optimises the parameters of the encoder, decoder and the soft attention (alignment) model by maximising the conditional likelihood of the target sentence given the source. The attention weights \(\alpha_{ij}\) are computed by a feed‑forward alignment network and are incorporated as differentiable nodes in the computational graph, allowing gradients to flow from the translation loss through the alignment scores back to the encoder and decoder. This unified objective replaces the traditional two‑stage approach where alignment is treated as a latent variable and learned separately.

types.explanation: It defines a novel algorithmic training procedure that integrates alignment learning into the overall loss.
It establishes a framework where attention (alignment) and translation are learned simultaneously in a single end‑to‑end model.

contribution_obj.name: Recurrent Attention Model (RAM) architecture
contribution_obj.description: The paper introduces a novel recurrent neural network architecture for visual processing that sequentially selects fixation locations, extracts a multi‑resolution retinal glimpse at each location, and integrates these glimpses over time with a core RNN. The hidden state of the RNN feeds a stochastic location network that chooses the next fixation and an action network that produces task‑specific outputs such as classification or control. Because computation is limited to the glimpses, the amount of processing is independent of the full image size while still providing translation invariance.

types.explanation: Proposes a completely new neural network architecture that combines recurrent processing with a learned visual attention mechanism.
Frames visual attention as a sequential decision problem implemented by a single RNN.
Reformulates visual processing as a partially observable Markov decision process (POMDP).

contribution_obj.name: End-to-end training of RAM with REINFORCE, learned baseline, and hybrid supervised loss
contribution_obj.description: The paper introduces a training procedure for the Recurrent Attention Model (RAM) that combines standard back‑propagation for differentiable components (glimpse, core, and action networks) with a REINFORCE policy‑gradient update for the stochastic location network. To reduce the high variance of the REINFORCE estimator, a learned baseline (value function) is trained jointly with the model. For classification tasks the final action output is also trained with a supervised cross‑entropy loss, yielding a hybrid objective that leverages both reinforcement and supervised signals.

types.explanation: Provides a novel training algorithm that merges back‑propagation with REINFORCE for attention models.
Specifies a concrete learning procedure for partially observable sequential visual models.

sections: Research Methods (Variance Reduction, Hybrid Supervised Loss)

52967399.c7.paper_title: BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding

contribution_obj.name: Open-source release of BERT code and pretrained checkpoints
contribution_obj.description: The authors publicly release a TensorFlow implementation of BERT together with the pretrained BERT‑BASE and BERT‑LARGE model checkpoints via a GitHub repository. The release includes all source files needed to reproduce the model architecture, pre‑training procedures, and fine‑tuning scripts. By providing the code and weights, the work enables reproducibility, rapid prototyping, and further research by the community without requiring re‑training from scratch.

types.explanation: Provides software artifacts (code and pretrained models) that can be directly used by other researchers.

1957433.c0.paper_title: GloVe: Global Vectors for Word Representation

contribution_obj.name: GloVe: Global Log‑Bilinear Regression Model for Word Embeddings
contribution_obj.description: The paper introduces GloVe, a novel word‑embedding model that directly factorizes a global word‑word co‑occurrence matrix using a weighted log‑bilinear regression objective. By modeling the logarithm of co‑occurrence counts with a weighted least‑squares loss, the method combines the statistical efficiency of global matrix‑factorization approaches with the ability of local context‑window models to capture linear substructures such as analogies. The resulting vectors encode semantic and syntactic regularities and achieve state‑of‑the‑art performance on analogy, similarity, and named‑entity‑recognition benchmarks.

types.explanation: Proposes a new model architecture for learning word representations.
Defines a new way to represent words as vectors derived from co‑occurrence statistics.
Provides a unified framework that bridges count‑based and prediction‑based embedding methods.

12730203.c6.paper_title: Linguistic Regularities in Sparse and Explicit Word Representations

contribution_obj.name: Explicit high‑dimensional sparse word vectors from Wikipedia using PPMI
contribution_obj.description: The authors build a traditional distributional representation by counting word–context co‑occurrences within a symmetric window of two tokens on the English Wikipedia corpus. They weight the raw counts with positive pointwise mutual information (PPMI) to obtain a |V|×|C| sparse matrix where each dimension corresponds to a positional context (e.g., "-2", "+1"). This explicit vector space serves as a reproducible baseline for relational similarity experiments and demonstrates that large‑scale sparse vectors can be constructed from publicly available data.

types.explanation: Creates a sparse, explicit word‑vector representation based on PPMI weighting.
Provides a reproducible pipeline for constructing high‑dimensional co‑occurrence vectors from a public corpus.

7478738.c1.paper_title: Linguistic Regularities in Continuous Space Word Representations

contribution_obj.name: Vector offset algorithm for word analogy solving
contribution_obj.description: The paper introduces a simple algorithm that solves word analogy questions of the form "a is to b as c is to ?" by computing the vector y = xb – xa + xc, normalising the resulting vector to unit length, and selecting the vocabulary word whose embedding has the highest cosine similarity to y. The method directly exploits the empirically observed constant offset property of word embeddings learned by a recurrent neural network language model. It provides a fast, language‑independent reasoning mechanism that can be applied to both syntactic and semantic analogy sets.

types.explanation: Introduces a new algorithmic procedure that uses vector arithmetic and cosine similarity to answer analogy queries.

1957433.c8.paper_title: GloVe: Global Vectors for Word Representation

contribution_obj.name: Open‑Source Implementation and Pre‑trained Word Vectors Release
contribution_obj.description: The authors publicly release the software for training the GloVe model together with pretrained word‑vector embeddings learned on several large corpora (Wikipedia 2010/2014, Gigaword 5, Common Crawl). The code, scripts, and vector files are hosted at http://nlp.stanford.edu/projects/glove/, enabling reproducibility and immediate downstream use by the research community.

5590763.c1.paper_title: Learning Phrase Representations using RNN Encoder–Decoder for Statistical Machine Translation

contribution_obj.name: Gated Recurrent Unit (reset and update gates)
contribution_obj.description: The paper introduces a novel recurrent hidden unit that augments the standard RNN state with two multiplicative gates: a reset gate r that controls how much of the previous hidden state is ignored when computing the candidate activation, and an update gate z that interpolates between the previous hidden state and the candidate activation, thereby adaptively remembering or forgetting information. The unit’s equations (5)–(8) are computationally simpler than the LSTM while providing comparable adaptive memory capabilities. This gated unit is employed in both the encoder and decoder of the proposed RNN Encoder–Decoder architecture.

types.explanation: Introduces a new recurrent hidden unit design that changes the architecture of RNNs.
Specifies novel gating computations and update equations for training the unit.

prerequisites.description: Understanding of the basic RNN recurrence h_t = f(h_{t-1}, x_t), the use of activation functions, and training via back‑propagation through time. This includes awareness of the vanishing‑gradient problem that motivates more sophisticated units.
prerequisites.explanation: The gated unit builds directly on the standard RNN recurrence and replaces its simple activation with gated computations.

contribution_obj.name: Leaky‑integration hidden units for longer temporal dependencies
contribution_obj.description: The paper augments standard recurrent neural networks by assigning a subset of hidden units leaky‑integration dynamics: ht,i = αi ht−1,i + (1 − αi) F_i(ht−1,xt), where αi is sampled uniformly from (0.02, 0.2). These slower‑changing units act as low‑pass filters, providing shorter effective gradient paths that mitigate vanishing gradients and allow information to persist over longer time‑scales. Experiments on polyphonic music and Penn Treebank language modeling demonstrate lower training and test loss compared to vanilla RNNs.

types.explanation: Modifies the standard recurrent architecture by adding leaky‑integration dynamics to a fraction of units.

7365802.c0.paper_title: ADADELTA: An Adaptive Learning Rate Method

contribution_obj.name: ADADELTA adaptive learning‑rate algorithm
contribution_obj.description: ADADELTA is a per‑dimension adaptive learning‑rate method that updates parameters using only first‑order gradient information. It maintains an exponentially decaying average of squared gradients (RMS[g]) for the denominator and an exponentially decaying average of past parameter updates (RMS[Δx]) for the numerator, yielding the unit‑consistent update Δx = - (RMS[Δx]_{t-1} / RMS[g]_{t}) * g_t. The algorithm removes the need for a manually tuned global learning rate, adds only trivial overhead to vanilla SGD, and is robust across network architectures, data modalities, and distributed training settings.

types.explanation: Introduces a novel optimization algorithm with a new adaptive learning‑rate scheme.
Frames adaptive learning‑rate computation as a unit‑consistent RMS‑based ratio.

6628106.c0.paper_title: Adam: A Method for Stochastic Optimization

contribution_obj.name: Adam: Adaptive Moment Estimation Optimization Algorithm
contribution_obj.description: Adam is a first‑order stochastic optimization method that computes per‑parameter learning rates using exponential moving averages of the gradient (first moment) and the squared gradient (second raw moment). The algorithm incorporates bias‑corrected moment estimates to eliminate initialization bias, yields updates invariant to diagonal rescaling of gradients, and bounds the effective step size by a global stepsize hyper‑parameter. Designed for non‑stationary objectives and sparse or noisy gradients, Adam requires only minimal additional memory and computation beyond standard stochastic gradient descent.

types.explanation: Introduces a novel adaptive optimization algorithm with a specific update rule.
Provides pseudo‑code and default hyper‑parameter settings for immediate use by practitioners.

202786778.c0.paper_title: PyTorch: An Imperative Style, High-Performance Deep Learning Library

contribution_obj.name: Imperative define‑by‑run deep learning library (PyTorch)
contribution_obj.description: The paper introduces PyTorch, a Python library that performs immediate (eager) execution of tensor operations while providing reverse‑mode automatic differentiation and GPU acceleration. By embracing Python's imperative programming model, models, data loaders, and optimizers can be written as ordinary Python code, enabling straightforward debugging and rapid experimentation. The authors demonstrate that careful engineering allows PyTorch to achieve performance comparable to static‑graph frameworks, challenging the belief that dynamic execution necessarily incurs a large speed penalty.

types.explanation: PyTorch is a released software system that enables deep‑learning model development.
The paper argues that dynamic eager execution need not sacrifice performance.

207847663.c2-2.paper_title: ERASER : A Benchmark to Evaluate Rationalized NLP Models

contribution_obj.name: Soft‑selection baseline models for rationalized predictions
contribution_obj.description: The paper implements a suite of soft‑selection baselines that assign continuous importance scores to each token. A BERT encoder followed by a bidirectional LSTM produces contextual token representations; token‑level attention, gradient‑based saliency, and LIME are applied to obtain soft importance scores. These scores are evaluated for agreement with human rationales and for faithfulness using the ERASER metrics. The models are trained on the ERASER benchmark and the implementation is released as open‑source code.

52967399.c0.paper_title: BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding

contribution_obj.name: Bidirectional Transformer encoder architecture (BERT)
contribution_obj.description: BERT introduces a multi‑layer Transformer encoder in which every token attends to both its left and right context through fully bidirectional self‑attention. The model combines WordPiece token embeddings, learned segment embeddings, positional embeddings, and special tokens ([CLS], [SEP]) to represent single sentences or sentence pairs uniformly. This architecture serves as a universal language representation that can be fine‑tuned with a minimal task‑specific output layer for a wide range of NLP tasks.

types.explanation: Proposes a new model architecture based on a bidirectional Transformer encoder.
Defines a novel way to encode text that captures full left‑right context at every layer.

sections: Appendix A.4: Comparison of BERT, ELMo, and OpenAI GPT

contribution_obj.name: Transformer: attention‑only encoder‑decoder architecture
contribution_obj.description: The paper presents the Transformer, a novel sequence‑to‑sequence model that replaces recurrent and convolutional layers with stacks of multi‑head self‑attention and position‑wise feed‑forward networks in both encoder and decoder. Positional information is injected via sinusoidal encodings, and the model employs residual connections, layer normalization, weight tying, dropout, label smoothing, and a warm‑up inverse‑sqrt learning‑rate schedule. This architecture achieves state‑of‑the‑art translation quality while dramatically reducing training time.

types.explanation: Introduces a completely new neural network architecture for sequence transduction.
Provides a high‑level design paradigm for building models solely on attention mechanisms.

3603249.c3.paper_title: Google's Neural Machine Translation System: Bridging the Gap between Human and Machine Translation

contribution_obj.name: Wordpiece sub‑word segmentation for open‑vocabulary NMT
contribution_obj.description: GNMT adopts a data‑driven wordpiece model that learns a fixed vocabulary of 8 k–32 k sub‑word units shared between source and target languages. The model uses a greedy algorithm to select wordpieces that maximize language‑model likelihood on the training corpus, yielding a deterministic segmentation for any word. This representation balances the flexibility of character models with the efficiency of word‑level models, enabling efficient handling of rare and out‑of‑vocabulary words while keeping sequence lengths manageable for the deep LSTM encoder‑decoder.

types.explanation: Introduces a novel sub‑word representation for neural machine translation.
Implements a greedy algorithm to learn the wordpiece vocabulary.

contribution_obj.name: ELMo: Deep Contextualized Word Representations
contribution_obj.description: The paper introduces ELMo (Embeddings from Language Models), a novel class of word representations that are computed as functions of the entire input sentence rather than fixed type embeddings. ELMo vectors are derived from the internal states of a deep bidirectional LSTM language model that is pretrained on a large monolingual corpus, enabling the representations to capture both complex syntactic/semantic characteristics and context‑dependent polysemy. The authors show that ELMo can be concatenated to any existing NLP architecture with minimal changes, providing a universal source of linguistic knowledge that improves state‑of‑the‑art performance on a wide range of downstream tasks. This contribution establishes a conceptual framework for using deep contextualized representations as a general paradigm in NLP.

types.explanation: Defines a novel way to encode words using deep, context‑dependent vectors derived from a language model.
Frames deep contextualized word representations as a general, task‑agnostic paradigm for NLP.

contribution_obj.name: Pretrained Bidirectional Language Model Architecture
contribution_obj.description: The authors design a two‑layer bidirectional LSTM language model that incorporates character‑level convolutional token embeddings, two highway layers, and a residual connection between the LSTM layers. The model is trained on the 1‑Billion Word Benchmark for ten epochs, achieving a forward‑backward perplexity of 39.7 while balancing capacity and computational cost. This pretrained biLM serves as the backbone for generating deep contextualized ELMo word representations, and the pretrained weights and extraction code are released publicly for reuse.

types.explanation: Proposes a specific deep biLM architecture with character convolutions, highway layers, and residual connections.
Provides a publicly available pretrained model and code for extracting ELMo vectors.

contribution_obj.name: Integrated Gradients attribution method
contribution_obj.description: The paper introduces Integrated Gradients, a feature attribution technique that computes the path integral of a network's gradients along the straight‑line segment from a baseline input to the actual input. The integral is approximated with a Riemann sum (e.g., 50 steps) using only standard gradient calls, so no network modification is required. Integrated Gradients provably satisfies the Sensitivity and Implementation Invariance axioms as well as the Completeness property, and can be implemented in any deep‑learning framework such as TensorFlow.

types.explanation: Introduces a novel algorithm for computing feature attributions based on path integrals of gradients.
Provides a lightweight implementation that can be added to existing deep‑learning codebases.

13029170.c0.paper_title: "Why Should I Trust You?" Explaining the Predictions of Any Classifier

contribution_obj.name: LIME: Local Interpretable Model‑agnostic Explanations
contribution_obj.description: LIME introduces a model‑agnostic explanation technique that approximates any black‑box classifier locally with an interpretable linear model defined over a human‑understandable representation (e.g., binary word presence). It generates perturbed samples around the instance of interest, weights them with an exponential kernel based on cosine distance, and fits a sparse linear model by selecting a small set of features via Lasso or forward selection and estimating their weights with weighted least squares. The resulting explanation highlights the most influential interpretable components, providing a faithful and intelligible rationale for the classifier’s prediction.

types.explanation: LIME proposes a novel algorithmic pipeline for generating local, model‑agnostic explanations.
It establishes a general framework for interpreting any classifier using an interpretable surrogate model.

All code and documentation are publicly available on GitHub, enabling reuse and further research.
